1: Psalm 85, a Korah psalm, moves between recollection of past restoration and petition for present renewal. רָצִיתָ יְהוָה אַרְצֶךָ שַׁבְתָּ שְׁבִית יַעֲקֹב (rāṣîtā yĕhwāh ʾarṣekā shab̈tā shĕbît yaʿăqōb, "O LORD, you showed favor to your land; you restored the fortunes of Jacob!") — rāṣāh: to be pleased with, to accept favorably (Day of Atonement language — Leviticus 1:4; Isaiah 40:2: "her iniquity is pardoned"). Shab̈tā shĕbît: the cognate accusative — "you restored the restoration of Jacob" (idiom for returning from captivity — Deuteronomy 30:3; Jeremiah 30:3; Job 42:10). The psalm's first three verses all use completed-action perfects — describing divine acts as done, laying them as the foundation for petition.
2: נָשָׂאתָ עֲוֹן עַמֶּךָ כִּסִּיתָ כָל-חַטָּאתָם סֶלָה (nāśāʾtā ʿăwōn ʿammekā kissîtā kol-ḥaṭṭāʾtām selāh, "you forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered over all their sin! Selah.") — two paired verbs: nāśāʾ ʿāwōn: "to lift/bear iniquity" (as the scapegoat bears Israel's sins — Leviticus 16:22; as the Servant of Isaiah 53:12 "bore the sin of many"). Kissāh ḥaṭṭāʾāh: "to cover sin" (Psalms 32:1: "blessed is the one whose sin is covered!" — Romans 4:7 cites Psalms 32:1 directly). The two great acts of atonement: lifting and covering — both perfect-tense completed divine acts. Selāh.
3: אָסַפְתָּ כָל-עֶבְרָתֶךָ הֱשִׁיבוֹתָ מֵחֲרוֹן אַפֶּךָ (ʾāsaptā kol-ʿebrātekā hĕshîbôtā mêḥărôn ʾappekā, "you withdrew all your wrath; you turned away from your fierce anger!") — ʿebrāh: overflow of wrath (the noun from ʿābar: to overflow); ḥărôn ʾap: the burning of the nostrils (Exodus 32:12). Both were gathered in (ʾāsap: to collect, withdraw) and turned back. This third completed-action verse is the memory that makes petition in vv4-7 possible: God who has done all this can do it again.
4: שׁוּבֵנוּ אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׁעֵנוּ (shûbênû ʾĕlōhê yishʿênû, "restore us again, O God of our salvation!") — the first petition: qal imperative of shûb: "cause us to return/restore us." The same verb that described Israel's physical return (shab̈tā shĕbît — v1) now turned back to God: cause the inner and national restoration that the physical return did not fully achieve. ʾĔlōhê yishʿênû: "God of our salvation" — the divine title that stakes the petition on God's own character as savior. וְהָפֵר כַּעַסְךָ עִמָּנוּ (wĕhāpêr kaʿăśekā ʿimmānû, "and set aside your displeasure against us!") — pārar (hiphil: to break, to annul, to cause to cease — used of breaking a covenant or decree).
5: הַלְעוֹלָם תֶּאֱנַף-בָּנוּ (halĕʿôlām teʾĕnap-bānû, "will you be angry with us forever?") — ʾānap: to be angry (Deuteronomy 1:37; Psalms 4:21; Psalms 9:20). The interrogative hal (surely not?) expects a negative answer. תִּמְשֹׁךְ אַפְּךָ לְדֹר וָדֹר (timmĕshōk ʾappĕkā lĕdōr wādōr, "will you extend your anger through every generation?") — māshak: to draw out, to prolong (to pull something along — as water drawn from a well). Anger drawn through generations contrasts with Exodus 34:6 (the divine character: "slow to anger" — ʾerek ʾappayim, literally "long-nostriled"). The petition presses the divine character against the present divine anger.
6: הֲלֹא-אַתָּה תָּשׁוּב תְּחַיֵּנוּ (hălōʾ-ʾattāh tāshûb tĕḥayyênû, "will you not revive us again, so that your people may rejoice in you?") — the interrogative-expectant: hălōʾ ʾattāh presses the divine identity as the one who revives. Ḥāyāh (piel: to give life, to revive — 2 Kings 13:21: the dead man touching Elisha's bones "revived and stood"; Hosea 6:2: "on the third day he will raise us up"). Tĕḥayyênû: "revive us" — the resurrection language used for national renewal and, in the prophets, for eschatological resurrection. וְעַמְּךָ יִשְׂמְחוּ-בָךְ (wĕʿammĕkā yiśmĕḥû-bāk, "that your people may rejoice in you!") — joy in God as the fruit of revival: the returning life expresses itself in rejoicing in the divine person, not merely in changed circumstances.
7: הַרְאֵנוּ יְהוָה חַסְדֶּךָ וְיֶשְׁעֲךָ תִּתֶּן-לָנוּ (harʾênû yĕhwāh ḥasdekā wĕyeshʿăkā titten-lānû, "Show us your steadfast love, O LORD, and grant us your salvation!") — rāʾāh (hiphil: to show, cause to see — to make the divine ḥesed visible and demonstrable). Ḥasdekā: your steadfast love (Lamentations 3:22). Yeshʿăkā: your salvation (the noun from yāshaʿ: to save — as in the name Jesus/Yeshua). The paired petition: make the love visible + give the salvation. The two are inseparable: showing the ḥesed and giving salvation are the same divine action.
8: אֶשְׁמְעָה מַּה-יְדַבֵּר הָאֵל יְהוָה (ʾeshĕmĕʿāh mah-yĕdabbêr hāʾêl yĕhwāh, "I will listen to what God the LORD says!") — a dramatic turn: the Psalmist falls silent from his petitions and adopts the prophetic listening posture. ʾEshĕmĕʿāh (cohortative: I will listen — the deliberate act of oriented attention): as Samuel (1 Samuel 3:9-10: "speak, for your servant is listening"), as Habakkuk (Habakkuk 2:1: "I will stand at my watchpost and listen"). כִּי-יְדַבֵּר שָׁלוֹם אֶל-עַמּוֹ וְאֶל-חֲסִידָיו (kî-yĕdabbêr shālôm ʾel-ʿammô wĕʾel-ḥăsîdāyw, "for he will speak peace to his people and to his faithful ones!") — shālôm: comprehensive wholeness, covenant-restoration (Isaiah 52:7: "how beautiful... 'Your God reigns'"). וְאַל-יָשׁוּבוּ לְכִסְלָה (wĕʾal-yāshûbû lĕkhislāh, "only let them not return to folly!") — kĕsîlāh: folly (from kĕsîl: the fool who refuses wisdom — Proverbs 1:32; Psalms 3:35). The peace is conditional: do not return to the foolishness of idolatry and covenant-breaking.
9: אַךְ-קָרוֹב לִירֵאָיו יִשְׁעוֹ (ʾak-qārôb lîrêʾāyw yishʿô, "surely his salvation is near to those who fear him!") — qārôb: near, close (the divine salvation described as spatially imminent — Psalms 34:7). לִשְׁכֹּן כָּבוֹד בְּאַרְצֵנוּ (lishkōn kābôd bĕʾarṣênû, "so that glory may dwell in our land!") — kābôd: the divine glory (the kābôd YHWH that filled the tabernacle — Exodus 40:34-35 — and the temple — 1 Kings 8:11 — and departed in the crisis of Ezek 10-11). The petition: may the glory that departed return and dwell (shākan). Ezekiel 43:1-5 promises its return. John 1:14 declares fulfillment: "the Word... tabernacled (ἐσκήνωσεν) among us, and we saw his glory (δόξαν)."
10: חֶסֶד-וֶאֱמֶת נִפְגָּשׁוּ צֶדֶק וְשָׁלוֹם נָשָׁקוּ (ḥesed-wĕʾĕmet nipgāshû ṣedeq wĕshālôm nāshāqû, "steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss!") — the most celebrated verse of Psalm 85, one of the great theological lines of the Psalter. Four divine covenant attributes personified as figures who meet and embrace. Nifgash (niphal of pāgash: to meet, to encounter — as in Exodus 4:24 where YHWH "met" Moses): ḥesed (covenant love) and ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth — the two paired throughout: Psalms 89:14; Genesis 24:27) meeting in greeting. Nāshaq: to kiss (Genesis 29:11; Psalms 45:15): ṣedeq (righteousness — the divine right-making, the justification) and shālôm (peace — the wholeness of restored relationship) kissing. Luther saw this as a prophecy of the incarnation: "Where could these four attributes meet except at the cross, where love and faithfulness met in the sacrifice of the Son, and righteousness and peace kissed in the reconciliation of God and humanity?" The verse is not allegory — it is the covenant vision of reality restored.
11: אֱמֶת מֵאֶרֶץ תִּצְמָח (ʾĕmet mêʾereṣ tiṣmaḥ, "faithfulness springs up from the earth!") — ṣāmaḥ: to sprout, to spring up (the agricultural verb for vegetation breaking through — Isaiah 45:8: "let righteousness spring up from the earth"). ʾĔmet: faithfulness/truth rising from the ground — the covenant faithfulness of the human community responding to the divine character. וְצֶדֶק מִשָּׁמַיִם נִשְׁקָף (wĕṣedeq mishshāmayim nishqāp, "and righteousness looks down from heaven!") — nishqāp (niphal of shāqap: to look down from a height — Numbers 21:20; Genesis 26:8). Heaven and earth in exchange: faithfulness rising from below; righteousness gazing down from above — the mutual meeting of human and divine in covenant fulfillment.
12: גַּם-יְהוָה יִתֵּן הַטּוֹב (gam-yĕhwāh yittên haṭṭôb, "yes, the LORD will give what is good!") — haṭṭôb: the good (with the definite article: the specific covenantal good that belongs to restoration — Psalms 84:11: "no good thing does he withhold"). וְאַרְצֵנוּ תִּתֵּן יְבוּלָהּ (wĕʾarṣênû tittên yĕbûlāh, "and our land will yield its harvest!") — yĕbûl: the produce, the yield (Leviticus 26:4,20; Deuteronomy 11:17 — harvest as the sign of covenant blessing or its absence). The restoration of v1 (God showed favor to the land) now extends to productive abundance: the land gives back what the curse had withheld.
13: צֶדֶק לְפָנָיו יְהַלֵּךְ (ṣedeq lĕpānāyw yĕhallêk, "righteousness will march before him!") — ṣedeq personified as the divine advance-guard: the one who goes before the divine warrior to prepare the way (as the ark went before Israel — Numbers 10:33; Joshua 3:6). The image of divine righteousness as forerunner/herald: Isaiah 58:8 ("your righteousness will go before you"); Isaiah 45:8 ("let righteousness spring up from the earth"). Paul's declaration in Romans 1:17 that the righteousness of God is being revealed (ἀποκαλύπτεται δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ) in the gospel echoes this precise imagery — the advance of divine ṣedeq into human history. וְיָשֵׂם לְדֶרֶךְ פְּעָמָיו (wĕyāśêm lĕderek pĕʿāmāyw, "and prepare a path for his steps!") — pĕʿāmîm: footsteps, the rhythmic advance of God's march into his restored world. Righteousness clears the road and lays the path for the divine arrival — the psalm that began by remembering a past restoration ends looking forward to the final arrival of God himself, preceded by his righteousness as herald.
